1: lāmāh ʾĕlōhîm zānaḥtā lāneṣaḥ — why, O God, have you cast us off forever? Zānaḥ (to reject, push away, cast off) is the communal lament's opening verb — the same word in Ps 44:9; 60:1. Lāneṣaḥ (forever, permanently) — the fear is not temporary abandonment but permanent divorce from the covenant relationship. yēʿshan ʾapĕkhā bĕṣōʾn marʿîtekhā — why does your anger smoke against the sheep of your pasture? The shepherd-flock metaphor (Ps 23) inverted: the shepherd's anger is destroying his own flock.
2: zĕkor ʿēdātekhā qānîtā qedem — remember your congregation which you acquired long ago. Qānāh (to acquire, create, possess) — the verb of creation theology (Gen 14:19,22; Prov 8:22); the congregation is God's own created possession. The rhetorical strategy: remind God of what he has already invested. gāʾaltā shēbet naḥălātekhā — which you redeemed as the tribe of your heritage. Gāʾal (kinsman-redemption) — the Exodus is the great redemption that grounds all subsequent appeals for rescue.
3: hārîmāh pĕʿāmêkhā lĕmashuʾôt nēṣaḥ — direct your steps to the perpetual ruins. The lament invites God to come and see what has been done to his sanctuary — to walk through the wreckage. kol-hērēaʿ ʾôyēb baqqōdesh — all the evil the enemy has done in the sanctuary. The sanctuary (qōdesh) is the place that belongs to God; its desecration is therefore a direct affront to divine honor, not merely a national disaster.
4: shāʾăgû ṣōrĕrêkhā bĕqereb môʿădêkhā — your foes have roared in the midst of your assembly place. The môʿēd (appointed meeting place, the sanctuary where God meets with Israel) is filled with the roar (shāʾag — the lion's roar) of enemies. śāmû ʾôtōtām ʾōtôt — they have set up their own signs as signs. The enemy's military standards (ʾōtôt) replace the divine signs (ʾōtôt) — the same word is used of YHWH's signs in the Exodus (Exod 10:1-2). Replacing divine signs with human signs is the defining sacrilege.
5: yiwwādaʿ kĕmēbîʾ lĕmāʿĕlāh — it seemed as if someone was raising axes. A difficult verse; the image is of wood-cutters attacking the carved temple panels — the very skilled labor that built the temple (1 Kgs 6) reversed in destruction. The craftsman who raised his axe to carve is invoked to image the vandal who raises his axe to destroy.
6: pittûḥêhā yaḥad bĕkashîl wĕkêlappôt yahaʾlômûn — its carved wood they broke down with hatchets and hammers. Pittûḥîm (carved works — the intricate temple paneling described in 1 Kgs 6:29-36) battered with kêlappôt (hammers, mauls). The detail makes the lament concrete: specific sacred objects destroyed by specific violent tools. The specificity is itself a form of testimony — witness to what was done.
7: shillĕḥû bāʾēsh miqdāshêkhā — they set your sanctuary on fire. The burning of the temple (miqdāsh) — the most extreme act of desecration (2 Kgs 25:9). lāʾāreṣ ḥillĕlû mishkan shĕmêkhā — they profaned to the ground the dwelling place of your name. Mishkan shĕmêkhā (dwelling place of your name) — the Deuteronomic theology of the divine name dwelling in the chosen place (Deut 12:5,11) makes this profanation an attack on the divine name itself, not merely on a building.
8: ʾāmĕrû bĕlibbām nînām yaḥad — they said in their hearts, 'We will oppress them altogether'. The enemies' interior speech — their deliberate program of total destruction. śārĕpû kol-môʿădê-ʾêl bāʾāreṣ — they burned all the meeting places of God in the land. Môʿădê-ʾêl (meeting places of God) — possibly the regional high places or local sanctuaries, or the proto-synagogues of exilic communities; the burning extends beyond Jerusalem to every place of assembly.
9: ʾôtōtênû lōʾ rāʾînû — we do not see our signs. The divine signs (ʾōtôt) — the Exodus miracles and the ongoing prophetic word — are absent. The word plays against v4: the enemy's signs have replaced the divine signs. ʾên-ʿôd nābîʾ — there is no longer a prophet. The cessation of prophetic word is the most devastating element of the lament: without a prophet there is no access to the divine will, no word of interpretation, no promise of duration. wĕlōʾ-ʾittānû yōdēaʿ ʿad-māh — and there is none among us who knows how long. Not even the duration of the silence is known — the silence is total.
10: ʿad-māttay ʾĕlōhîm yĕḥārēph ṣār — how long, O God, will the enemy mock? Ḥāraph (to reproach, revile, challenge) — the enemy's taunting is a theological challenge: if God cannot protect his sanctuary and his people, his name is dishonored. yĕnāʾēṣ ʾôyēb shĕmĕkhā lāneṣaḥ — shall the enemy revile your name forever? The divine reputation becomes the argument for divine action — God must act for his own name's sake (cf. Ezek 36:22-23).
11: lāmmāh tāshîb yādĕkhā wîmînekhā — why do you hold back your hand, your right hand? The yad yāmîn (right hand) is the hand of divine power — the Exodus weapon-hand (Exod 15:6,12). miqqereb ḥêqĕkhā kallēh — from the midst of your bosom, destroy! God's power is folded in his lap like a concealed weapon — the plea is for him to draw and act. The image pictures divine restraint as physical containment that must be released.
12: wĕʾĕlōhîm malkî miqqedem — yet God is my King from of old. The pivot of the lament — the move from complaint to the basis of appeal. The antiquity of God's kingship (miqqedem: from ancient times, from the beginning) provides historical ground for current petition. pōʿēl yĕshûʿôt bĕqereb hāʾāreṣ — working salvation in the midst of the earth. Past salvations are the pattern for future ones; what God has done in history he can do again.
13: ʾattāh phōrartā vĕʿozzĕkhā yām — you divided the sea by your might. The Exodus sea-crossing narrated in creation-mythological terms: shabbartā rāʾshê tannînîm ʿal-hammāyim — you shattered the heads of the sea monsters on the waters. Tannîn (sea dragon, chaos monster) echoes Canaanite mythology (Lothan/Leviathan, the serpent with seven heads in Ugaritic texts). YHWH's defeat of the sea monsters is both historical (Exodus) and cosmological (creation ordering). If God defeated the chaos of Egypt and the primordial sea, he can defeat Babylon.
14: ʾattāh rāṣaṣtā rāʾshê liwyāthān — you crushed the heads of Leviathan. Liwyāthān (Leviathan — from lāwāh: to coil, twist) is the primordial sea creature of chaos (Job 41; Isa 27:1). The plural heads echo the seven-headed dragon of Ugaritic mythology. tittĕnennû maʾăkhāl lĕʿam lĕṣiyyîm — you gave him as food for the creatures of the wilderness. The chaos monster becomes food — an ironic reversal. YHWH's sovereignty over chaos is absolute: even Leviathan feeds others at his disposal.
15: ʾattāh bāqaʿtā maʿyān wānaḥal — you split open springs and wadis. The water-from-the-rock (Exod 17; Num 20) and the Jordan crossing (Josh 3) stand behind this verse — YHWH produces water in the desert and stops water in the river. ʾattāh hôbashtā naharôt ʾêtān — you dried up ever-flowing streams. Nĕharôt ʾêtān (ever-flowing, perennial rivers) — YHWH stops what cannot naturally stop. Both miracles demonstrate mastery over water in its full range of behaviors.
16: lĕkhā yôm ʾaf-lĕkhā lāylāh — yours is the day, yours also the night. Creation theology: day and night belong to YHWH — neither is the domain of rival powers or foreign deities. ʾattāh hăkhînôtā māʾôr wāshāmesh — you established the light and the sun. The sun is not a deity (contra Egypt's Ra) but a created instrument under YHWH's control. The creation confession forms the ground for the appeal to the Creator to act on behalf of his creation.
17: ʾattāh hiṣṣabtā kol-gebûlôt ʾāreṣ — you established all the boundaries of the earth. Gebûl (boundary, border) — God as the one who sets the limits of nations (cf. Deut 32:8; Acts 17:26). qayiṣ wāḥōrep ʾattāh yĕṣartām — summer and winter, you made them. The seasonal boundaries — another dimension of divine ordering. All of vv13-17 establish the Creator's credentials for the specific petition of vv18-23: the one who ordered creation can restore the sanctuary.
18: zĕkhār-zōʾt ʾôyēb ḥērap YHWH — remember this, O LORD, how the enemy has mocked. The appeal shifts from creation theology to divine reputation: the enemy's taunting is directed against YHWH, not merely against Israel. wĕʿam nābāl nēʾăṣû shĕmekā — and a foolish people has despised your name. Nābāl (fool — the one who lives as if God does not exist, cf. Ps 14:1) — the enemy's mockery is practical atheism. What is at stake is the divine name.
19: ʾal-tittēn lĕḥayyat nefesh tôrekhā — do not deliver the soul of your dove to the wild beasts. Tôr (turtledove) — a gentle, vulnerable bird used in sacrifice (Lev 1:14; 5:7; Luke 2:24), here as a metaphor for Israel. The turtledove is helpless against predators — the image of Israel's radical vulnerability without divine protection. ḥayyat ʿăniyyêkhā ʾal-tishkāḥ lāneṣaḥ — do not forget the life of your poor forever. The two terms tôrekhā (your dove) and ʿăniyyêkhā (your poor) equate the nation with the poor — those who have nothing but God.
20: habbēṭ labberît — have regard for the covenant. The covenant (berît) is the final ground of appeal — not Israel's merit but the divine commitment. kî māleʾû maḥăshakkê-ʾereṣ neʾôt ḥāmās — for the dark places of the land are full of the habitations of violence. Maḥăshakkê-ʾereṣ (the dark places of the land) — the caves and hiding places of the exilic condition; darkness as the condition of the covenant people when the covenant seems forgotten.
21: ʾal-yāshob dāk niklām — let not the downtrodden be put to shame. Dāk (crushed, oppressed — from dākhāh: to crush) — those already beaten down must not also face the shame of unanswered prayer. ʾānî wĕʾebyôn yĕhallĕlû shĕmekhā — let the poor and needy praise your name. Their future praise is the purpose of present intervention — the community's worship is contingent on divine rescue. The appeal to God's desire to be praised is itself a motive for action.
22: qûmāh ʾĕlōhîm rîbāh rîbekhā — arise, O God, plead your own cause. The repeated root rîb (legal dispute, lawsuit) — both noun and verb: rîbāh rîbekhā (plead your plea, fight your fight). The appeal reverses the ordinary legal frame: God is not merely petitioned to take Israel's side but called to defend his own case against those who dishonor him. God is plaintiff, not merely judge. zĕkhor ḥerpātekhā minnî-nābāl kol-hayyôm — remember how the fool mocks you all day long.
23: ʾal-tishkāḥ qôl ṣōrĕrêkhā — do not forget the clamor of your foes. The final appeal: God must not forget the noise of those who oppose him. shaʾăwat qāmêkhā ʿōlāh tāmîd — the tumult of those who rise against you goes up continually. Tāmîd (continually) — the unceasing noise of opposition to God is the psalm's final word, hanging without resolution, awaiting divine response. The lament ends in suspension — a theological posture of waiting that trusts the God of v12 to act.
